html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Twenty-Seven — Complete

Eikha

EIKHA

The Lamentations of Yirmeyahu

Chapters 1 through 5

How Lonely Sits the City — The Steadfast Love of YHWH Never Ceases — They Are New Every Morning — Restore Us to Yourself O YHWH That We May Be Restored

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — Qumran Lamentations Scroll

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

Masoretic Text

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

EIKHA — THE LAMENTATIONS — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 5

EIKHA — CHAPTER 1 How Lonely Sits the City — She Weeps Bitterly in the Night — Yerushalayim Sinned Grievously — YHWH Is in the Right — Look and See If There Is Any Sorrow Like My Sorrow — Is It Nothing to You All Who Pass By

1 How lonely sits the city that was full of people. How like a widow has she become, she who was great among the nations. She who was a princess among the provinces has become a slave.

2 She weeps bitterly in the night, with tears on her cheeks. Among all her lovers she has none to comfort her. All her friends have dealt treacherously with her. They have become her enemies.

3 Yehudah has gone into exile because of affliction and because of great servitude. She dwells now among the nations, but finds no resting place. Her pursuers have all overtaken her in the midst of her distress.

4 The roads to Tziyon mourn, for none come to the appointed feasts. All her gates are desolate. Her priests groan. Her virgins have been afflicted, and she herself suffers bitterly.

5 Her foes have become the head. Her enemies prosper, because YHWH has afflicted her for the multitude of her transgressions. Her children have gone away, captives before the foe.

6 From the daughter of Tziyon all her majesty has departed. Her princes have become like deer that find no pasture. They fled without strength before the pursuer.

7 Yerushalayim remembers in the days of her affliction and of her wandering all the precious things that were hers from days of old. When her people fell into the hand of the foe, and there was none to help her, her foes gloated over her. They mocked at her downfall.

8 Yerushalayim sinned grievously. Therefore she became filthy. All who honored her despise her, for they have seen her nakedness. She herself groans and turns her face away.

9 Her uncleanness was in her skirts. She took no thought of her future. Therefore her fall is terrible. She has no comforter. O YHWH, behold my affliction, for the enemy has triumphed.

10 The enemy has stretched out his hands over all her precious things. For she has seen the nations enter her sanctuary, those whom you forbade to enter your congregation.

11 All her people groan as they search for bread. They trade their treasures for food to revive their strength. Look, O YHWH, and see, for I am despised.

12 Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was brought upon me, which YHWH inflicted on the day of his fierce anger.

13 From on high he sent fire. Into my bones he made it descend. He spread a net for my feet. He turned me back. He has left me stunned, faint all the day long.

14 My transgressions were bound into a yoke. By his hand they were fastened together. They were set upon my neck. He caused my strength to fail. The Lord gave me into the hands of those whom I cannot withstand.

15 YHWH rejected all my mighty men in my midst. He summoned an assembly against me to crush my young men. YHWH has trodden as in a winepress the virgin daughter of Yehudah.

16 For these things I weep. My eyes flow with tears. For a comforter is far from me, one to revive my spirit. My children are desolate, for the enemy has prevailed.

17 Tziyon stretches out her hands, but there is none to comfort her. YHWH has commanded against Ya'akov that his neighbors should be his foes. Yerushalayim has become a filthy thing among them.

18 YHWH is in the right, for I have rebelled against his word. But hear, all you peoples, and see my suffering. My young women and my young men have gone into captivity.

19 I called to my lovers, but they deceived me. My priests and elders perished in the city, while they sought food to revive their strength.

20 Look, O YHWH, for I am in distress. My stomach churns. My heart is wrung within me, because I have been very rebellious. In the street the sword bereaves. In the house it is like death.

21 They heard my groaning, yet there is none to comfort me. All my enemies have heard of my trouble. They are glad that you have done it. You have brought the day you announced. Now let them be as I am.

22 Let all their evildoing come before you, and deal with them as you have dealt with me because of all my transgressions. For my groans are many, and my heart is faint.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Dead Sea Scrolls preserve Eikha among the scrolls found at Qumran. The Lamentations scroll confirms the canonical text. Eikha is the covenant formal covenant mourning over the destruction of Yerushalayim and the covenant temple — the very covenant structures whose construction and dedication are the covenant climax of Divrei HaYamim. The covenant acrostic structure of Chapters 1, 2, and 4 — each verse beginning with successive letters of the covenant Hebrew alphabet — is the covenant literary form of covenant comprehensive covenant grief.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Eikha Chapter 1 in full. Verse 12 — is it nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was brought upon me, which YHWH inflicted on the day of his fierce anger. The covenant direct address to the covenant passerby is the covenant most haunting covenant rhetorical move in the covenant chapter: the covenant destroyed city speaks to everyone who passes by and demands covenant acknowledgment of her covenant unprecedented covenant suffering.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 1 in full. Verse 18 — YHWH is in the right, for I have rebelled against his word. The covenant acknowledgment at the center of the covenant lament: the covenant city does not claim covenant innocence. The covenant suffering is acknowledged as covenant deserved — and yet the covenant grief is real and the covenant cry for covenant divine attention is urgent. Eikha holds covenant honest covenant acknowledgment of covenant guilt and covenant authentic covenant grief simultaneously.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — how lonely sits the city that was full of people. How like a widow has she become. The covenant opening word eikha — how — is the covenant cry of the covenant book: not why but how. The covenant question is not covenant theological but covenant existential. The covenant city that was the covenant center of the covenant covenant community's covenant life now sits in covenant solitude like a covenant widow. The covenant widowhood metaphor positions the covenant city as one whose covenant intimate covenant partner — YHWH — has withdrawn.

EIKHA — CHAPTER 2 How YHWH Has Set the Daughter of Tziyon Under a Cloud — He Has Swallowed Up Without Mercy — YHWH Has Done What He Purposed — He Has Thrown Down Without Pity — Arise Cry Out in the Night — Pour Out Your Heart Like Water Before the Face of YHWH

1 How YHWH in his anger has set the daughter of Tziyon under a cloud. He has cast down from heaven to earth the splendor of Yisra'EL. He has not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger.

2 YHWH has swallowed up without mercy all the habitations of Ya'akov. In his wrath he has broken down the strongholds of the daughter of Yehudah. He has brought down to the ground in dishonor the kingdom and its rulers.

3 He has cut down in fierce anger all the might of Yisra'EL. He has withdrawn from them his right hand in the face of the enemy. He has burned like a flaming fire in Ya'akov, consuming all around.

4 He has bent his bow like an enemy, with his right hand set like a foe. And he has killed all who were delightful in our eyes in the tent of the daughter of Tziyon. He has poured out his fury like fire.

5 YHWH has become like an enemy. He has swallowed up Yisra'EL. He has swallowed up all its palaces. He has laid in ruins its strongholds, and he has multiplied in the daughter of Yehudah mourning and lamentation.

6 He has laid waste his booth like a garden, laid in ruins his meeting place. YHWH has made Tziyon forget appointed feast and Shabbat, and in his fierce indignation has spurned king and priest.

7 YHWH has scorned his altar, disowned his sanctuary. He has delivered into the hand of the enemy the walls of her palaces. They raised a clamor in the house of YHWH as on the day of an appointed feast.

8 YHWH determined to lay in ruins the wall of the daughter of Tziyon. He stretched out the measuring line. He did not restrain his hand from destroying. He caused rampart and wall to lament. They languished together.

9 Her gates have sunk into the ground. He has ruined and broken her bars. Her king and princes are among the nations. The law is no more, and her prophets find no vision from YHWH.

10 The elders of the daughter of Tziyon sit on the ground in silence. They have thrown dust on their heads and put on sackcloth. The young women of Yerushalayim have bowed their heads to the ground.

11 My eyes are spent with weeping. My stomach churns. My bile is poured out to the ground because of the destruction of the daughter of my people, because infants and babies faint in the streets of the city.

12 They cry to their mothers, Where is bread and wine, as they faint like a wounded man in the streets of the city, as their life is poured out on their mothers' bosom.

13 What can I say for you, to what compare you, O daughter of Yerushalayim? What can I liken to you, that I may comfort you, O virgin daughter of Tziyon? For your ruin is vast as the sea. Who can heal you?

14 Your prophets have seen for you false and deceptive visions. They have not exposed your iniquity to restore your fortunes, but have seen for you oracles that are false and misleading.

15 All who pass along the way clap their hands at you. They hiss and wag their heads at the daughter of Yerushalayim: Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty, the joy of all the earth?

16 All your enemies open their mouths against you. They hiss and gnash their teeth. They cry: We have swallowed her. Ah, this is the day we longed for. Now we have found it. We have seen it.

17 YHWH has done what he purposed. He has carried out his word, which he commanded long ago. He has thrown down without pity. He has made the enemy rejoice over you and exalted the might of your foes.

18 Their heart cried to YHWH. O wall of the daughter of Tziyon, let tears stream down like a torrent day and night. Give yourself no rest. Your eyes have no respite.

19 Arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the night watches. Pour out your heart like water before the face of YHWH. Lift your hands to him for the lives of your children, who faint for hunger at the head of every street.

20 Look, O YHWH, and see. With whom have you dealt thus? Should women eat the fruit of their womb, the children of their tender care? Should priest and prophet be killed in the sanctuary of YHWH?

21 In the dust of the streets lie the young and the old. My young women and my young men have fallen by the sword. You have killed them in the day of your anger, slaughtering without pity.

22 You summoned as if to a feast day my terrors on every side, and on the day of the anger of YHWH none escaped or survived. Those whom I held and raised, my enemy destroyed.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Eikha fragments confirm the text. Chapter 2 is the covenant most theologically direct of the five covenant laments: YHWH is named as the covenant agent of the covenant destruction seventeen times. The covenant city does not attribute its covenant destruction to covenant Babylonian covenant military covenant power alone but to YHWH's covenant own covenant action. YHWH has become like an enemy — verse 5. This covenant theological directness is the covenant foundation of covenant authentic covenant lament.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 19 — arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the night watches. Pour out your heart like water before the face of YHWH. Lift your hands to him for the lives of your children. The covenant call to covenant night-prayer on behalf of the covenant children is the covenant pastoral covenant heart of the covenant chapter: amid the covenant comprehensive covenant theology of YHWH as covenant destroyer, the covenant invitation to covenant prayer to the same YHWH remains.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 13 — what can I say for you, to what compare you, O daughter of Yerushalayim? For your ruin is vast as the sea. Who can heal you? The covenant acknowledgment that the covenant destruction exceeds all covenant capacity for covenant comfort or covenant comparison: the covenant comforter cannot find covenant adequate covenant words because the covenant grief exceeds all covenant existing covenant categories. The covenant absence of comfort is itself the covenant lament.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — your prophets have seen for you false and deceptive visions. They have not exposed your iniquity to restore your fortunes, but have seen for you oracles that are false and misleading. The covenant indictment of the covenant false prophets embedded within the covenant lament: the covenant destruction is partly attributed to the covenant failure of the covenant prophetic covenant institution to speak covenant honest covenant diagnostic covenant truth. The covenant prophets who whitewashed — as Iyov said — contributed to the covenant catastrophe.

EIKHA — CHAPTER 3 I Am the Man Who Has Seen Affliction — He Has Driven Me and Made Me Walk in Darkness — He Has Made Me Dwell in Darkness — But This I Call to Mind and Therefore I Have Hope — The Steadfast Love of YHWH Never Ceases — YHWH Is Good to Those Who Wait for Him — Let Us Test and Examine Our Ways

1 I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his wrath.

2 He has driven me and made me walk in darkness and not in light.

3 Surely against me he turns his hand again and again the whole day long.

4 He has made my flesh and my skin waste away. He has broken my bones.

5 He has besieged and enveloped me with bitterness and tribulation.

6 He has made me dwell in darkness like the dead of long ago.

7 He has walled me about so that I cannot escape. He has made my chains heavy.

8 Though I call and cry for help, he shuts out my prayer.

9 He has blocked my ways with blocks of stones. He has made my paths crooked.

10 He is a bear lying in wait for me, a lion in hiding.

11 He turned aside my steps and tore me to pieces. He has made me desolate.

12 He bent his bow and set me as a target for his arrow.

13 He drove into my kidneys the arrows of his quiver.

14 I have become the laughingstock of all peoples, the object of their taunts all day long.

15 He has filled me with bitterness. He has sated me with wormwood.

16 He has made my teeth grind on gravel, and made me cower in ashes.

17 My soul is bereft of peace. I have forgotten what happiness is.

18 So I say, My endurance has perished. So has my hope from YHWH.

19 Remember my affliction and my wanderings, the wormwood and the gall.

20 My soul continually remembers it and is bowed down within me.

21 But this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope:

22 The steadfast love of YHWH never ceases. His mercies never come to an end.

23 They are new every morning. Great is your faithfulness.

24 YHWH is my portion, says my soul. Therefore I will hope in him.

25 YHWH is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him.

26 It is good that one should wait quietly for the salvation of YHWH.

27 It is good for a man that he bear the yoke in his youth.

28 Let him sit alone in silence when it is laid on him.

29 Let him put his mouth in the dust — there may yet be hope.

30 Let him give his cheek to the one who strikes, and let him be filled with insults.

31 For YHWH will not cast off forever.

32 But, though he cause grief, he will have compassion according to the abundance of his steadfast love.

33 For he does not afflict from his heart or grieve the children of men.

34 To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the earth,

35 To deny a man justice in the presence of the Most High,

36 To subvert a man in his lawsuit, the Lord does not approve.

37 Who has spoken and it came to pass, unless the Lord has commanded it?

38 Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that good and bad come?

39 Why should a living man complain, a man, about the punishment of his sins?

40 Let us test and examine our ways, and return to YHWH.

41 Let us lift up our hearts and hands to Creator in heaven.

42 We have transgressed and rebelled, and you have not forgiven.

43 You have wrapped yourself with anger and pursued us, killing without pity.

44 You have wrapped yourself with a cloud so that no prayer can pass through.

45 You have made us scum and garbage among the peoples.

46 All our enemies open their mouths against us.

47 Panic and pitfall have come upon us, devastation and destruction.

48 My eyes flow with rivers of tears because of the destruction of the daughter of my people.

49 My eyes will flow without ceasing, without respite,

50 Until YHWH from heaven looks down and sees.

51 My eyes cause me grief at the fate of all the daughters of my city.

52 I have been hunted like a bird by those who were my enemies without cause.

53 They flung me alive into the pit and cast stones on me.

54 Water closed over my head. I said, I am lost.

55 I called on your name, O YHWH, from the depths of the pit.

56 You heard my plea: Do not close your ear to my cry for help.

57 You came near when I called on you. You said, Do not fear.

58 You have taken up my cause, O Lord. You have redeemed my life.

59 You have seen the wrong done to me, O YHWH. Judge my cause.

60 You have seen all their vengeance, all their plots against me.

61 You have heard their taunts, O YHWH, all their plots against me.

62 The lips and thoughts of my assailants are against me all the day long.

63 Behold their sitting and their rising. I am the object of their taunts.

64 You will repay them, O YHWH, according to the work of their hands.

65 You will give them dullness of heart. Your curse will be on them.

66 You will pursue them in anger and destroy them from under your heavens, O YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran Eikha fragments confirm the text. Chapter 3 is the covenant heart of the book — the longest covenant chapter with sixty-six verses in a triple acrostic structure, each letter of the covenant Hebrew alphabet generating three consecutive verses. The covenant pivot of verse 21-24 — but this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope: the steadfast love of YHWH never ceases, his mercies never come to an end, they are new every morning — is the covenant most celebrated covenant statement of covenant hope in the entire covenant canon of covenant lament.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 22-23 — the steadfast love of YHWH never ceases. His mercies never come to an end. They are new every morning. Great is your faithfulness. The covenant declaration of covenant divine covenant steadfast love — hesed — in the midst of the covenant most comprehensive covenant description of covenant suffering in the covenant canon: the covenant steadfast love is not discovered in spite of the covenant suffering but through the covenant dark passage of the covenant lament itself.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 3 in full. Verse 40-41 — let us test and examine our ways, and return to YHWH. Let us lift up our hearts and hands to Creator in heaven. The covenant call to covenant corporate covenant self-examination and covenant return: the covenant individual lament of the first half of the covenant chapter becomes the covenant communal covenant call to covenant repentance in the second half. The covenant book's covenant theology requires both covenant honest covenant grief and covenant honest covenant self-examination.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 21 — but this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope. The covenant structural pivot of the entire book: twenty verses of covenant comprehensive covenant suffering-under-YHWH's-hand are followed by this covenant single covenant turn of covenant memory and covenant hope. The covenant pivot is not covenant denial of the covenant suffering but covenant deliberate covenant memory: the covenant speaker calls to mind something that was not in the covenant foreground of the covenant suffering. The covenant hope is not covenant spontaneous but covenant intentional — a covenant act of covenant remembering.

EIKHA — CHAPTER 4 How the Gold Has Grown Dim — The Precious Sons of Tziyon — Those Who Once Ate Delicacies Perish in the Streets — The Iniquity of the Daughter of My People Was Greater Than the Sin of Sodom — The Punishment of Your Iniquity O Daughter of Tziyon Is Accomplished

1 How the gold has grown dim, how the pure gold is changed. The holy stones lie scattered at the head of every street.

2 The precious sons of Tziyon, worth their weight in fine gold, how they are regarded as earthen pots, the work of a potter's hands.

3 Even jackals offer the breast. They nurse their young. But the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.

4 The tongue of the nursing infant sticks to the roof of its mouth for thirst. The children beg for food, but no one gives to them.

5 Those who once ate delicacies perish in the streets. Those who were brought up in purple embrace ash heaps.

6 For the iniquity of the daughter of my people has been greater than the sin of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, and no hands were wrung for her.

7 Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk. Their bodies were more ruddy than coral. The beauty of their form was like sapphire.

8 Now their face is blacker than soot. They are not recognized in the streets. Their skin has shriveled on their bones. It has become as dry as wood.

9 Happier were the victims of the sword than the victims of hunger, who wasted away, pierced by lack of the fruits of the field.

10 The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children. They became their food during the destruction of the daughter of my people.

11 YHWH gave full vent to his wrath. He poured out his hot anger, and he kindled a fire in Tziyon that consumed its foundations.

12 The kings of the earth did not believe, nor any of the inhabitants of the world, that foe or enemy could enter the gates of Yerushalayim.

13 This was for the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed in the midst of her the blood of the righteous.

14 They wandered, blind, through the streets. They were so defiled with blood that no one was able to touch their garments.

15 Away. Unclean. People cried at them. Away. Away. Do not touch. So they became fugitives and wanderers. People said among the nations, They shall stay with us no longer.

16 YHWH himself has scattered them. He will regard them no more. No honor was shown to the priests, no favor to the elders.

17 Our eyes failed, ever watching vainly for help. In our watching we watched for a nation which could not save.

18 They dogged our steps so that we could not walk in our streets. Our end drew near. Our days were numbered, for our end had come.

19 Our pursuers were swifter than the eagles in the heavens. They chased us on the mountains. They lay in wait for us in the wilderness.

20 The breath of our nostrils, YHWH's anointed, was caught in their pits, of whom we said, Under his shadow we shall live among the nations.

21 Rejoice and be glad, O daughter of Edom, you who dwell in the land of Utz. But to you also the cup shall pass. You shall become drunk and strip yourself bare.

22 The punishment of your iniquity, O daughter of Tziyon, is accomplished. He will keep you in exile no longer. But your iniquity, O daughter of Edom, he will punish. He will uncover your sins.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 4 returns to the acrostic structure and focuses on the covenant contrast between the covenant before and the covenant after: the covenant precious sons of Tziyon who were worth their weight in fine gold — verse 2 — are now regarded as earthen pots. The covenant gold has grown dim — verse 1. The covenant covenant community's covenant elite have been reduced to the covenant ash heap. The covenant covenant reversal of covenant fortune is total.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 20 — the breath of our nostrils, YHWH's anointed, was caught in their pits, of whom we said, Under his shadow we shall live among the nations. The covenant anointed king as the covenant breath of the covenant community's covenant nostrils: the covenant Davidic king who was the covenant center of the covenant covenant community's covenant political and covenant theological covenant identity has been captured. The covenant covenant community's covenant protective covenant shadow has been removed.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 22 — the punishment of your iniquity, O daughter of Tziyon, is accomplished. He will keep you in exile no longer. The covenant promise within the covenant lament: the covenant punishment is finished — accomplished — tam. The covenant exile will not continue forever. The covenant final verse of the covenant fourth chapter is the covenant first note of covenant eschatological covenant hope in the covenant book: the covenant punishment that is accomplished is the covenant punishment that has an covenant end.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — this was for the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed in the midst of her the blood of the righteous. The covenant specific covenant diagnosis of the covenant destruction: the covenant covenant failures of the covenant prophetic and covenant priestly covenant leadership are identified as the covenant primary covenant cause. The covenant pattern is consistent throughout the covenant canon: covenant prophets who spoke falsely and covenant priests who shed covenant innocent covenant blood brought the covenant judgment that produced the covenant lament.

EIKHA — CHAPTER 5 Remember O YHWH What Has Befallen Us — The Crown Has Fallen From Our Head — For This Our Heart Has Become Sick — But You O YHWH Reign Forever — Why Do You Forget Us Forever — Restore Us to Yourself O YHWH

1 Remember, O YHWH, what has befallen us. Look, and see our disgrace.

2 Our inheritance has been turned over to strangers, our homes to foreigners.

3 We have become orphans, fatherless. Our mothers are like widows.

4 We must pay for the water we drink. The wood we get must be bought.

5 Our pursuers are at our necks. We are weary. We are given no rest.

6 We have given the hand to Egypt, and to Assyria, to get bread enough.

7 Our fathers sinned, and are no more. And we bear their iniquities.

8 Slaves rule over us. There is none to deliver us from their hand.

9 We get our bread at the risk of our lives, because of the sword in the wilderness.

10 Our skin is hot as an oven with the burning heat of famine.

11 Women are raped in Tziyon, young women in the cities of Yehudah.

12 Princes are hung up by their hands. No respect is shown to the elders.

13 Young men are compelled to grind at the mill, and boys stagger under loads of wood.

14 The old men have left the city gate, the young men their music.

15 The joy of our hearts has ceased. Our dancing has been turned to mourning.

16 The crown has fallen from our head. Woe to us, for we have sinned.

17 For this our heart has become sick. For these things our eyes have grown dim,

18 For Mount Tziyon which lies desolate. Jackals prowl over it.

19 But you, O YHWH, reign forever. Your throne endures to all generations.

20 Why do you forget us forever? Why do you forsake us for so long?

21 Restore us to yourself, O YHWH, that we may be restored. Renew our days as of old —

22 Unless you have utterly rejected us, and you remain exceedingly angry with us.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Qumran fragments confirm the text. Chapter 5 is the covenant community prayer — the only chapter in Eikha that is not an acrostic. The covenant communal covenant voice replaces the covenant individual covenant voice of Chapter 3: the covenant we of the covenant exiled covenant community speaks in covenant unison. The covenant final two verses — verse 21-22 — are the covenant most covenant theologically daring covenant ending of any book in the covenant canon: a covenant plea for covenant restoration followed by the covenant terrifying covenant conditional — unless you have utterly rejected us.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 21 — restore us to yourself, O YHWH, that we may be restored. Renew our days as of old. The covenant prayer for covenant restoration is the covenant most concentrated covenant request in the covenant book: the covenant community does not ask merely for covenant return from covenant exile but for covenant restoration to YHWH — hashivenu elekha YHWH — the covenant relationship itself is the covenant primary covenant need. The covenant material covenant restoration will follow from the covenant relational covenant restoration.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic text preserves Chapter 5 in full. Verse 19-22 — but you, O YHWH, reign forever. Your throne endures to all generations. Why do you forget us forever? The covenant juxtaposition of covenant eternal covenant divine covenant sovereignty with covenant experienced covenant divine covenant abandonment is the covenant final covenant theological statement of the covenant book: YHWH's covenant throne is eternal — and YHWH seems to have forgotten the covenant community. The covenant book ends without the covenant tension resolved.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 22 — unless you have utterly rejected us, and you remain exceedingly angry with us. The covenant most stark covenant ending in the covenant canon: the covenant book of covenant lament ends not with covenant consolation but with covenant open covenant question about covenant possible covenant permanent covenant rejection. The covenant rabbinical tradition instructed that verse 21 — restore us to yourself — be repeated after verse 22 in synagogue reading, so that the covenant book does not end on the covenant most devastating covenant possibility. The covenant canonical covenant text ends with the covenant unresolved covenant tension.

EIKHA — COMPLETE

Volume Twenty-Seven — The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

RESTORE US TO YOURSELF O YHWH THAT WE MAY BE RESTORED

RENEW OUR DAYS AS OF OLD

Eikha 5:21
